Notice Of Motion In Support Of Request For Both Issue Sanctions And Monetary Sanctions In An Amount Not Less Than 6785 (Motions)
Off Calendar Jul-13-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

RE: Plaintiffs PETERSON, MARTIN, & REYNOLDS LLP, et al, Motion in Support of Request for Both Issue Sanctions and Monetary Sanctions.

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel, or Interested Person.

The court adopts its tentative ruling. Plaintiffs/Cross-Defendants' motion for sanctions is denied. Plaintiffs/Cross-Defendants bring their motion pursuant to CCP 128.7(b)(2) - (3), on the grounds that Defendants/Cross-Complainants' MSA lacks legal support, because (1) their entire argument that a breach of fiduciary duty has been established is premised on the fiction that the overruling of a demurrer is a ruling on the merits and (2) they included no expert declaration regarding whether Plaintiffs breached any fiduciary duty, which precluded them from prevailing on their MSA as a matter of law. Under section 128.7, an attorney or unrepresented party who files a pleading, motion, or similar paper must sign the document, which impliedly certifies the pleading has legal and factual merit. (CCP 128.7(a).) The signature reflects the certification of the attorney or unrepresented party that the pleading is not being presented for an improper purpose; the legal contentions are warranted by law or nonfrivolous argument for extension, modification or reversal of existing law; the allegations and factual contentions have evidentiary support or are likely to have such support after a reasonable opportunity to further investigate; and the denials of factual contentions are warranted by the evidence. (CCP 128.7(b); In re Marriage of Reese & Guy, (1999) 73 Cal.App.4th 1214, 1220; Barnes v. Department of Corrections (1999) 74 Cal.App.4th 126, 130.) The attorney or unrepresented party is subject to sanctions for violation of this certification. (In re Marriage of Reese & Guy, supra, at p. 1220.) The evidentiary burden to escape sanctions under CCP 128.7 is "light." Counsel only need to have "made a reasonable inquiry into the facts and entertained a good faith belief in the merits of the claim." (Kumar v. Ramsey (2021) 71 Cal.App.5th 1110, 1126.) Defendants' counsel shows there was a reasonable inquiry into the facts and demonstrates their good faith belief in the merits of the claim. (Opp. 8-12.) On the heels of the Court of Appeal affirming Judge Ulmer's decision to overrule Plaintiffs demurrers and denying the writ of mandate, Defendants counsel believed the statute of limitations issue was resolved as a matter of law. (Id. at 5:7-10.) However, when the motion for summary adjudication was before the court on July 3, 2023, it was revealed that Plaintiffs had obtained additional evidence in discovery. (See July 3, 2023 Order.) Additionally, Plaintiffs concede that "California law does not require an expert witness to prove a breach of the standard of care." MPA, 17:12-13.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not reported. (302/RBU)

Off Calendar Jul-03-2023 Off Calendar

MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION .
Off Calendar Jul-03-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

RE: Defendants CATHERINE HANIFF, PETER MCDONALD, and VICTORIA HANIFF's MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION.

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel or Interested Person.

The court adopts its tentative ruling. Defendants CATHERINE HANIFF, PETER MCDONALD, and VICTORIA HANIFF's MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION is denied. Cross-Complainants argue that summary adjudication should be granted because each element for a negligent breach of fiduciary duty is met, there are no triable issues of material fact and defendants have no defenses. (Not., 10-17.) Cross-Defendants negotiated a settlement agreement for cross-complainants which included "crucial" terms that an independent board would be implemented and or the matter would proceed to trial. (MPA, 2:28, 3:1-2.) The language of the settlement agreement reads as follows: "The parties agree to explore retaining an independent board in good faith to act as buffer between the condominium owners." (C. Haniff Decl., Ex. B.) No independent board was ever implemented, and the case never went to trial. (UMFs 7-8.) "The elements of a cause of action for breach of fiduciary duty are: (1) existence of a fiduciary duty; (2) breach of the fiduciary duty; and (3) damage proximately caused by the breach." Ash v. N. Am. Title Co., (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 1258, 1276. Cross-Complainants argue that there was a breach because cross-defendants misrepresented to them that the settlement agreement required the implementation of an independent board and if that did not happen, that they would have the option to try the underlying action. (MPA, 10:18-20.) Cross-Defendants argue that statute of limitations defense has not been negated, there if no expert declaration to establish breach, and there is no triable issue regarding causation. (Opp., 2:8-16.) Here, upon additional evidence obtained in discovery, a triable issue of material fact exists as to when the statute of limitations began to run. (Opp., 13:13-15, See AMF 11.) Cross-Defendants argue that the actual injury occurred in May 2018 when cross-complainants were incurring attorneys' fees at a new firm. (See Graft Decl., Ex. 12.) Cross-Defendants cite to Truong v. Glasser (2009) 181 Cal.App.4th 102, 115, for the proposition that cross-complainants first sustained actual injury when they obtained and were obligated to pay new counsel to file a lawsuit, seeking to escape the consequences of the attorneys' misconduct-thus triggering the limitations period under CCP 340.6. (Opp. 13:1-13.) A statute of limitations defense cannot properly be resolved by summary adjudication when facts are susceptible to more than one legitimate inference. (See State Compensation Ins. Fund v Superior Court (2010) 184 CA4th 1124, 1132-1134.)

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Clerk: R. Woods, Not Reported. (302/RBU)

Off Calendar May-25-2022 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

CROSS-DEFENDANTS PETERSON, MARTIN, & REYNOLDS LLP, A LIMITED, P. KURT PETERSON and JOSEPH CHARLES' DEMURRER to CROSS COMPLAINT.

Cross-defendants' demurrer is ordered off calendar for noncompliance with CCP 430.41, which requires that the parties conduct a "meet and confer in person or by telephone" before a demurrer is filed. CCP 430.41 also requires the moving party to file and serve a declaration stating "the means by which the demurring party met and conferred with the party who filed the pleading." The parties are ordered to meet and confer immediately and cross-defendants' response to the cross-complaint is now due by June 22, 2022.
